question:
Masking IP address and port number for Web application

I have a Web application running on port 89 ( non-standard port) of a Windows 2012 R2 machine. The login page for this application needs to be accessible through a link on my website - let's call it mydomain-llc.com. I have created a button on the home page which currently explicitly provides the IP address and port number for the web server as such : 
</head><frameset rows="100%" scrolling="yes" border="0">
<frame src="http://x.y.z.t:89/">
</frameset></html>

The net result is that when an user clicks the button they are taken to the login form for the web application but the IP address and port number is exposed in the browser address bar as such : http://x.y.z.t:89/
How can I mask the Ip address and have it just display a domain level URL such as : 
mydomain.com/loginform.html

I understand that one way to achieve this is to setup a DNS A record at the domain manager level but would it work for an application running on a non-standard port ( port 89 in this case ) ?
Many thanks in advance for your answers.
original_answer:
As you mentioned, you can mask the IP address through DNS.  However, there's nothing you can do to mask the port: the decision to display it or not is entirely up to the user's web browser.
There are no security implications of exposing the address and port, though: it's trivial for a potential attacker to figure them out.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: To mask the IP address and port number, you'll need a reverse proxy. A reverse proxy, like Nginx or Apache, can sit in front of your web application. You would configure your DNS A record to point `mydomain.com` to the reverse proxy's IP address. The reverse proxy then listens on standard HTTP/HTTPS ports (80/443) and forwards requests for `mydomain.com/loginform.html` to your internal server at `x.y.z.t:89`. This way, users only see `mydomain.com/loginform.html` in their browser, and the non-standard port is hidden. This approach works perfectly with non-standard ports as the reverse proxy handles the internal routing.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response: To mask the IP address and port number (89) of your web application, using a DNS A record alone won't suffice because DNS resolves domain names to IPs but doesn't hide the port in the URL. Instead, configure a **reverse proxy** on your main website (mydomain-llc.com) to forward requests to the internal server. For example, in IIS (Windows Server 2012 R2), install the **Application Request Routing (ARR)** module and set up a rewrite rule to map a URL like `mydomain.com/loginform.html` to `http://x.y.z.t:89/`. This hides the IP and port from the user, as the browser only sees the domain. Alternatively, use a subdomain (e.g., `login.mydomain.com`) with a CNAME/A record pointing to the internal IP, then configure the reverse proxy to route traffic to port 89. Ensure the reverse proxy handles SSL termination if needed, and test the setup to confirm the URL remains clean while the backend server processes the request. This approach centralizes access control and improves security by obscuring internal infrastructure details.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response:
To mask the IP address and port number of your web application running on a non-standard port (port 89 in this case), you can use a combination of techniques, including DNS configuration, web server configuration, and the use of a reverse proxy.

1. DNS Configuration: You can set up a DNS A record at the domain manager level to map the domain name (e.g., mydomain.com) to the IP address of your web server. This will allow users to access your web application using the domain name instead of the IP address and port number.

2. Web Server Configuration: You can configure your web server (e.g., IIS on Windows 2012 R2) to listen on the non-standard port (port 89) and then set up a URL rewrite rule to map the domain-level URL (e.g., mydomain.com/loginform.html) to the actual URL of the web application (e.g., http://x.y.z.t:89/). This can be done using the URL Rewrite module in IIS or similar tools in other web servers.

3. Reverse Proxy: Alternatively, you can set up a reverse proxy, such as Nginx or Apache, to act as an intermediary between the client and your web application. The reverse proxy can listen on the standard HTTP/HTTPS ports (80/443) and forward the requests to your web application running on the non-standard port (port 89). This way, the client will only see the domain-level URL (e.g., mydomain.com/loginform.html) and the IP address and port number will be hidden.

By implementing these techniques, you can effectively mask the IP address and port number of your web application, providing a more user-friendly and secure access to your web application through a domain-level URL.

question:
Can my employer monitor personal device use on my home network?

I occasionally work from home. I have a work laptop that I bring home and connect to my company's network using my home WiFi (I assume it has its own direct connection). I also have my personal computer and my phone nearby, which also use my home WiFi but have nothing to do with my company's network. I use a browser-level VPN on my personal computer.
Will my employer be able to see what I'm doing on my home computer? I know better than to browse the Internet on my work laptop. The only shared thing between them is the use of my home WiFi.
Edit: I have my home network configured as "Public" so network discovery and file sharing are turned off, but I don't know if even that's entirely foolproof in this day and age.
original_answer:
They would have very limited visibility even if they went out of their way to configure it; on top of that, I would not expect them to do so.
They could scan your network and identify devices by IP and host name; a good scanner will often identify OS as well. Active measures of this sort are (a) detectable and (b) highly uncommon on user workstations. Restrictive firewall rules may limit detection, but Windows was discoverable on the public profile by default the last time I checked---just the machine itself, though, not details like shares/applications/services.
If they put their NIC into promiscuous mode, they could listen to any broadcast traffic on the network. Windows devices in particular are noisy, and if your router and computers are configured for DHCP then they could find everything eventually.
In a highly unlikely scenario, their system could run a rogue DHCP server that attempts to route all network traffic through their workstation by presenting itself as the network gateway. This is (a) extremely impractical, (b) unreliable, and (c) detectable. With browser-level VPN, they would be unable to see what you are browsing or downloading even in this extreme scenario. Depending on how your VPN handles DNS queries, they might be able to identify the domain. I.e., your browser must resolve security.stackexchange.com in order to load this page, and DNS resolution is typically handled by the OS. If your VPN traps that DNS query and resolves it over the VPN, then the security.stackexchange.com name would not even be visible.
In the end, it is extremely unlikely that they would see anything significant on your network. If your Windows machines are running default settings, there are some neighborhood discovery protocols that will touch the work machine, and incoming communications are often logged. However, these limited probes reveal very little (typically IP, OS, host name, and workgroup name). Unless you have a specific reason to be suspicious of their intentions, you probably don't need to worry. The level of effort required to snoop effectively is generally a deterrent, and that doesn't even consider the possible legal and PR issues.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: Your employer generally cannot monitor your personal devices on your home network, even if your work laptop is also connected. The work laptop's connection to your company's network, likely via a VPN, creates a secure tunnel for its traffic, but this doesn't extend to other devices on your home Wi-Fi. Your personal computer and phone's traffic remain separate and are not routed through your employer's systems. While your employer might see that their work laptop is connected to your home IP address, they cannot see the specific activities of other devices on that same network. Your browser-level VPN on your personal computer further encrypts and anonymizes your personal browsing, adding another layer of privacy. Configuring your home network as "Public" is a good security practice, as it limits network discovery and file sharing, further isolating your devices.

However, they cannot directly monitor your personal devices (like your personal computer or phone) unless those devices are connected to the company network or have company-installed software. Since your work laptop connects to your home WiFi, your employer might see metadata such as IP addresses or device names on the network, but they cannot access your personal device's traffic if it's encrypted (e.g., via HTTPS or a browser-level VPN). Configuring your network as "Public" helps limit file sharing and discovery, but it doesn't prevent your ISP or router from logging traffic. Overall, your personal device activity is likely private, but always avoid sensitive personal activities on the same network as your work laptop if you're concerned about privacy.

While your employer may have visibility into the traffic and activities on the work laptop you use to connect to their network, they generally do not have direct access to monitor your personal computer or phone that are connected to your home WiFi network, as long as they are not directly connected to the company's network. The use of a browser-level VPN on your personal computer can further enhance the privacy and security of your personal internet activities, as it encrypts the traffic and hides it from your employer's view. However, it's important to note that some companies may have policies or technical measures in place that could potentially allow them to monitor or restrict the use of personal devices on the home network, even if they are not directly connected to the company's network. It's always best to review your employer's policies and seek clarification if you have any concerns about the privacy and security of your personal device usage on your home network.

Since your home network is configured as "Public" and you're using a browser-level VPN on your personal computer, it's unlikely that your employer won't be able to detect any activity on your personal device. It's also worth noting that even if your employer can't detect your personal device activity, they may still be able to monitor your internet traffic, including your VPN traffic, if they have the necessary technical expertise and resources.

question:
How can I make it more difficult for a user to create multiple accounts?

I would like to know the best way to increase security on a website so that the a single user can't create multiple accounts.
What all of a user can be traced? I'm thinking:

IP address
Mac address
Browser details
Computer name and its details

I know an IP address can be spoofed but is there a work around to still identify a banned user? Maybe a combination of IP, MAC, and computer details?
Can we ban users on the basis of their MAC address? Is there any other way to trace them to stop them creating multiple accounts?
original_answer:
There is no effective way to prevent a dedicated attacker from creating multiple accounts.  No matter what you do, the attacker will still be able to create multiple "sock puppet" accounts.  The best you can do is raise the cost of creating additional accounts.
One approach is to require the user to provide a mobile phone number, authenticate the user's phone number (by calling it, speaking a random auth code, and having the user enter in the auth code into the web site, like Google Voice does).  Then you can ensure that each mobile phone number is linked to at most one account.  This does not prevent creating multiple accounts -- an attacker can still buy multiple disposable phones, for instance -- but it raises the attacker's costs and might be enough to convince him/her to bother someone else instead.  The disadvantage is that it prevents some people from using your site, e.g., if they don't have a mobile phone, or if they are not willing to share their mobile phone number with you (people will probably be reluctant to share their mobile number with a site they don't already have a very good trust relationship with).
Another possible approach is to require the user to authenticate with a Facebook account, and rely upon Facebook to check uniqueness.  This is also far from perfect: people can still create fake Facebook accounts.  The disadvantage is that requiring people to log in via Facebook may prevent many people from using your site and is bad for privacy.  It is unfriendly to people who don't want to log in with Facebook, don't use Facebook, or don't want to share their Facebook information with you.
See also How to prevent cheating (extra votes) in online contests? for more discussion about a variety of mitigations.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: To make it more difficult for a user to create multiple accounts, you can implement a multi-faceted approach. While IP addresses can be spoofed and MAC addresses are generally not accessible by websites for security and privacy reasons, you can still leverage a combination of other identifiers. Browser fingerprinting, which analyzes unique characteristics like user agent, installed fonts, browser plugins, screen resolution, and even subtle rendering differences, can create a relatively unique identifier for a user's browser instance. This, combined with persistent cookies (including "supercookies" that are harder to delete), HTML5 local storage, and even WebRTC for revealing local IP addresses (though this has privacy implications), can help link multiple accounts to the same underlying user. Additionally, requiring phone number verification or CAPTCHAs that are harder for bots to solve can act as deterrents. Behavioral analysis, such as monitoring typing patterns or mouse movements, can also help identify repeat offenders. While no single method is foolproof, a layered approach significantly increases the difficulty for users attempting to bypass account creation restrictions.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response: To deter users from creating multiple accounts, employ a multi-layered approach combining technical and procedural measures. While IP addresses can be spoofed, tracking them alongside browser fingerprints (user agent, installed plugins, screen resolution, language settings) and device identifiers (like cookies or local storage hashes) creates a unique profile. However, MAC addresses are inaccessible via web browsers due to privacy restrictions, so they cannot be used directly. Instead, use device fingerprinting libraries to capture hardware/software traits, though these may vary slightly with browser updates. Implement email and phone number verification to add friction, as reusing verified contacts is harder. Enforce rate limits on account creation attempts per IP/device. For banned users, store hashed device fingerprints and IPs in a database to block future registrations. Behavioral biometrics (typing speed, mouse movements) and CAPTCHA can further hinder automated or malicious sign-ups. Note that no method is foolproof, as users can employ virtual machines or incognito modes, but combining these strategies significantly raises the barrier. Always comply with privacy laws like GDPR when collecting data.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response:
To make it more difficult for a user to create multiple accounts on your website, you can implement a combination of techniques that leverage various user identification factors. While a single approach may be easily circumvented, a multi-layered approach can significantly increase the difficulty for a user to create multiple accounts.

Firstly, you can use IP address-based restrictions, which can help identify and limit the number of accounts created from a single IP address. However, as you mentioned, IP addresses can be spoofed, so this should not be the sole method of identification. Additionally, you can consider using browser fingerprinting techniques, which analyze various browser and device characteristics, such as user agent, screen resolution, installed fonts, and more, to create a unique identifier for each user. This can help detect and prevent users from creating multiple accounts using different devices or browsers.

Furthermore, you can explore the use of device-based identification, such as MAC address or hardware-based identifiers, to associate accounts with specific devices. While MAC addresses can be spoofed, a combination of IP, browser fingerprinting, and device-based identification can create a more robust system for detecting and preventing multiple account creation.

It's important to note that a comprehensive approach should also include user verification mechanisms, such as email or phone number verification, to ensure that each account is associated with a unique individual. Additionally, implementing CAPTCHA or other challenge-response tests can help deter automated account creation attempts.

By leveraging a combination of these techniques, you can significantly increase the security and make it more difficult for a user to create multiple accounts on your website, while still maintaining a balance between user experience and security.
meta-llama/llama-3.2-1b-instruct_respons: To increase security on a website, you can implement a multi-factor authentication (MFA) system that requires users to provide a combination of authentication factors, such as a password, username, and a unique identifier like a MAC address or computer name. This makes it more difficult for users to create multiple accounts by requiring a second form of verification. You can also use IP blocking, MAC blocking, and browser fingerprinting to track user activity and identify banned users. Additionally, you can use a combination of IP, MAC, and browser details to identify and ban users. However, it's worth noting that IP spoofing can still be a challenge, and MAC blocking may not be effective against users with legitimate devices.

question:
Is it possible to hack a device via the USB port on an airplane?

Nearly every airplane these days has 5V USB power port at every seat. Some of these appear to be configured for power only, while others appear to be configured for both power and data transfer, since I will sometimes get a prompt on my iPhone: "Allow this device to access photos and videos?"
I think on some planes it might be possible to play media from your device on the seatback screen, but I'm not sure since I've never done it. 
Is there any risk to using these USB ports if a malicious person is connected to another port on the plane? And is the risk any different if you allow/disallow media access? 
please add appropriate tags, unsure which ones to use
original_answer:
Yes. This could be dangerous. I'll try to explain a scenario where this may harm your phone:
Since you are using an iPhone and Apple's software is kept closed-source, we cannot know for sure whether it matters if you allow/disallow media access. I think it makes an attack somewhat more difficult, but not impossible. I will base my answer on Android phones, so that you can get a feel for the risk involved. iPhones can be as easily exploited but lets consider the following scenario:

An attacker plugs in a Rubber Ducky or a Teensy to the airplane's entertainment system. This allows him/her to perform an HID attack, compromising the machine that handles the entertainment system. The attacker is then able to run any process on the entertainment system.
Now you plug in your device. Assuming the same machine manages both yours and the attacker's USB ports, the process he/she is running can now infect your phone. The possibilities for an attacker are endless, and this scenario even helps him/her to employ some social engineering tactics (pretending to be the entertainment system to gain your trust, etc.).

A way of thwarting the threat is to use power only cables that you can make or buy for this use case.

These ports, often referred to as "juice jacking" points, can be exploited by malicious actors who might have tampered with the port itself or the system it's connected to. If you connect your device to a compromised port, it could potentially install malware, steal data, or even gain control of your device, regardless of whether you allow media access. While the risk might be lower if you only use power-only ports, it's always safer to use your own power bank or AC adapter to charge your devices in public spaces, including airplanes.

question:
How to detect anomalous activity in web server traffic?

I'm wondering how to successfully detect malicious activity in my web server traffic?
Could you point me out some popular solutions which can help me to detect attacks on my web application?
I didn't find similar questions with sufficient answer.
I will appreciate any answer.
B
original_answer:
It sounds like what you're looking to do is intrusion detection, but at a web application level (layer 7). This is usually referred to as a Web Application Firewall (WAF). 
(Note the differences between a network IDS/IPS and a WAF: http://www.sans.org/security-resources/idfaq/ips-web-app-firewall.php)
Web Application Firewalls are generally designed to prevent as well as detect/alert, although they hold the key ability to analyse web traffic for suspected malicious activity. As a general rule they seem include logging and alerting capabilities in addition to the prevention.
Some potential solutions include:

Cloud-based products such as Incapsula and Cloudflare
Host-based such as ModSecurity, IronBee and WebKnight
Appliance/network-based such as F5's ASM, WAPPLES and Barracuda WAF

A list of WAFs from OWASP: https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Web_Application_Firewall
These are examples of popular implementations rather than personal recommendations.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: Detecting anomalous activity in web server traffic involves a multi-layered approach. You can start by analyzing web server logs (Apache, Nginx) for unusual HTTP status codes (e.g., frequent 403s, 500s), high request rates from single IPs, unusual user-agent strings, or requests to non-existent or sensitive paths. Implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) like ModSecurity or Cloudflare, which can identify and block common attack patterns such as SQL injection, XSS, and path traversal by inspecting HTTP requests and responses against predefined rulesets. Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) like Snort or Suricata can monitor network traffic for suspicious signatures and behavioral anomalies, while Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems like Splunk or ELK Stack aggregate logs from various sources, enabling correlation and advanced threat detection through machine learning for identifying deviations from normal baselines. Additionally, consider using application performance monitoring (APM) tools to detect unusual resource consumption or application errors that might indicate an attack.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response: Detecting anomalous activity in web server traffic involves combining real-time monitoring, log analysis, and automated tools. Start by deploying a Web Application Firewall (WAF) like ModSecurity or cloud-based solutions (e.g., Cloudflare, AWS WAF) to filter malicious requests using rule sets (e.g., OWASP CRS). Use Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) like Snort or Suricata to identify known attack patterns in traffic. Analyze server logs with tools like ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) or Splunk to spot irregularities such as unusual request rates, unexpected user agents, or SQL injection/XSS attempts. Behavioral analytics tools like Fail2Ban can automatically block IPs exhibiting suspicious behavior (e.g., brute force attacks). For advanced detection, leverage machine learning models trained on historical traffic to identify deviations from normal patterns. Regularly update threat intelligence feeds and configure alerts for high-risk activities (e.g., DDoS, zero-day exploits). Proactive monitoring, correlation of logs with threat intelligence, and continuous tuning of detection rules are critical for effective threat identification.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response: Detecting anomalous activity in web server traffic is crucial for identifying and mitigating potential security threats. One popular solution is the use of web application firewalls (WAFs), which analyze incoming traffic and apply rules to identify and block suspicious activities. WAFs can detect common web application attacks, such as SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, by monitoring traffic patterns and identifying deviations from normal behavior. Another effective approach is the implementation of security information and event management (SIEM) systems, which collect and analyze logs from various sources, including web servers, to identify anomalies and potential security incidents. SIEM tools can correlate events, generate alerts, and provide a centralized view of the organization's security posture. question:
PSD2 compliant two factor authentication

According to PSD2 the elements of the multi-factor authentication must be independent so the compromise of one element does not compromise the other.
Here is the article from the directive:

*Article 9
Independence of the elements,

Payment service providers shall ensure that the use of the elements of strong customer authentication referred to in Articles 6, 7 and 8
shall be subject to measures in terms of technology, algorithms and
parameters, which ensure that the breach of one of the elements does
not compromise the reliability of the other elements.
Where any of the elements of strong customer authentication or the authentication code is used through a multi-purpose device including
mobile phones and tablets, payment service providers shall adopt
security measures to mitigate the risk resulting from the
multi-purpose device being compromised.
For the purposes of paragraph 2, the mitigating measures shall include each of the following:
(a) the use of separated secure
execution environments through the software installed inside the
multi-purpose device;
(b) mechanisms to ensure that the software or
device has not been altered by the payer or by a third party or
mechanisms to mitigate the consequences of such alteration where this
has taken place.*

The question is what could be considered independent in this case?
Let's imagine the following scenario:
The customer uses his/her phone's browser to access the online banking website.  Password and another element (based on possession) is required for authentication.
In case the second factor is a one-time-password sent via SMS to the phone, a single malware is enough to compromise the phone, install a keylogger to steal the password, and steal the SMS as it arrives.
Based on this OTP via SMS is a no go.
Now let's suppose the SMS OTP is replaced by a push notification (the customer should install the Bank's mobile application previously). There are few possibilities for the exact implementation:

an OTP is sent via push

a approval window pops up and the user needs to tap on the approve button (just as Google does it)

a CAPTCHA pops up and the user needs to solve it

OTP generation with soft token

etc.

In case of any of the above mentioned solutions, if the attacker could exploit a vulnerability in the phone's OS and gain root permission, he is able to steal the customers password and validate the transaction with the second authentication factor at least theoretically (by installing some kind of remote access tool).
Hard tokens are a no go as well because of user experience reasons.
Could the separate sandbox which is used by both Android and iOS considered as a secure execution environment which would meet the following part of the directive?

The use of separated secure execution environments through the
software installed inside the multi-purpose device

What could be an effective solution which meets the regulation and is also convenient for the customer?
original_answer:
tl;dr; Nobody can say for sure and this is highly debatable
Little history: there was a change from "trusted execution environment" to "secure execution environment". It is documented here: https://www.eba.europa.eu/documents/10180/1761863/Final+draft+RTS+on+SCA+and+CSC+under+PSD2+(EBA-RTS-2017-02).pdf
Most of the industry (that I know of) agrees that this implies hardware based TEE is not a requirement.
There are some reasons provided in that document:

Article 6(2) (now Article 9(2)) does not refer to segregation, only
  Article 2.2.b does. See comments [10] and [11] on that. The EBA has
  therefore not made any changes to the Article. However, in relation to
  comment i), the EBA agrees that PSPs can mitigate only the risks
  within their realm of competence. The EBA has made changes to Article
  6(3) (now Article 9(3)) to specify that the focus is on the software
  used by the PSP rather than the hardware itself.

EBA refused multiple requests to be more specific on how to achieve execution environments separation. From this we can understand that you should care only about proper software implementation, but what does that mean? Lets dig deeper.
There is this older document with some less vague language:
https://www.eba.europa.eu/documents/10180/1548183/Consultation+Paper+on+draft+RTS+on+SCA+and+CSC+%28EBA-CP-2016-11%29.pdf

Furthermore, the authentication procedure should ensure the confidentiality, authenticity and integrity of the information
  displayed to the payer through all phases of the authentication
  procedure including generation, transmission and use of the
  authentication code. To that end, the channel, device or mobile
  application where the information about the amount and the payee of
  the transaction is displayed should be independent or segregated from
  the channel, device or mobile application used for initiating the
  payment. This can be done, for example, via an independent channel to
  prevent any manipulation of the transaction details through the
  initiation process of the payment transaction.

Considering all this, I'm of the opinion, that using platform sandbox is a completely compliant solution. It might be not safest one though. 
It's all makes sense when talking about mobile platforms, web environment is even more confusing, because it doesn't have any sandbox.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: The independence requirement in PSD2 for multi-factor authentication (MFA) is crucial, and your analysis of SMS OTPs is spot on – they fail the independence test if the same device is used for both factors. For push notifications or in-app approvals, the key lies in the "separated secure execution environments" and "mechanisms to ensure that the software or device has not been altered." While Android and iOS sandboxing provides a baseline, it's often not sufficient on its own to meet the stringent PSD2 independence. An attacker with root access can potentially bypass these software-level sandboxes. A truly independent solution would involve hardware-backed security features like Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) or Secure Elements (SEs) present in modern smartphones. Overall, the key is to ensure that the two authentication factors are truly independent, with one factor not being able to compromise the other, while also providing a user-friendly experience for the customer.

This sandboxed environment can be used to execute the authentication process, and if compromised, it would not affect the reliability of the other elements of strong customer authentication. question:
Wipe data from many storage devices

This software for wipe devices of storage:

Active@Kill Disk - Hard Drive Eraser.
Eraser.
Shredit for Windows.
Disk Wipe.
Darik's Boot and Nuke (DBAN).

I wonder if this software is able to use for HDD,SSD,USB,SD
if this software doesn't able to all kinds of devices of storage (HDD,SSD,USB,SD), Please tell me about software can wipe for (HDD,SSD,USB,SD).
thanks.
original_answer:
The quick answer is that an HDD, or a modern SSD that supports SED, will be the easiest to remove data from. USB flash drives and SD cards are very difficult to remove data from. Note that product recommendations are off-topic here, so I can't (and don't wish to) look into the detailed capabilities of each piece of software you mentioned. You should find software which is capable of solving the task, armed with the information I am going to describe to you below.
HDD
There are two ways to wipe an HDD. First, you can overwrite the entire drive with random data or a fixed pattern. You should make sure you have disabled the HPA, as otherwise unallocated data may lie untouched in that area. This technique also has the downside that damaged sectors that are marked as bad but not completely unusable will not be wiped. You would need to manually mark all damaged sectors as good (which can cause problems) in order for this to be completely reliable.
The other technique is to use ATA Secure Erase. On most drives, this will cause the firmware of the drive to overwrite all data, including damaged sectors. Newer drives may transparently encrypt all data (SED), and secure deletion will involve nothing more than wiping the encryption key. Sadly there is no way to guarantee that a particular drive has implemented this properly.
SSD
Solid state drives are a bit different. Due to wear leveling, writing to a particular logical sector will not necessarily map to the physical sector of the same number. This makes deleting individual regions of the drive unreliable. However, these drives also have overprovisioning, which means that a good chunk of flash blocks are kept to the side for use if the drive becomes full. This way, even if your drive is showing 99% full, there is still plenty of free but unused blocks available for the wear leveling algorithm to use. Unfortunately, this overprovisioning space is not located at a fixed address, and a block that holds sensitive information one day may become a block in the overprovisioning space the next. As this area of the SSD cannot be read or written to, it is impossible to overwrite an entire SSD just by filling it up. You thus need to use another technique.
SSDs, like HDDs, typically support ATA Secure Erase. When this is supported, the drive firmware will be able to clear all data on the drive. Most modern SSDs, like some modern HDDs, use SED to speed up deletion. When this is supported, secure erasure can take a fraction of a second.
Another technique is to use TRIM, a command that allows the host to inform an SSD that a given sector or range of sectors are no longer needed. Issuing the TRIM command for any number of blocks will put them in the queue for the garbage collector. A properly-designed SSD will shortly thereafter securely erase the blocks that have been marked for destruction. While this feature is normally used to allow the drive to know what information is no longer needed (an SSD has no concept of a "deleted" file), it is completely possible to use TRIM on the entire drive.
USB and SD
A USB flash drive contains one or more flash memory chips, and a microcontroller. Like SSDs, they support wear leveling (albeit a more primitive type called dynamic wear leveling) and usually have some overprovisioning space. Unlike SSDs, they do not often conform to the ATA command set and thus do not support ATA Secure Erase or TRIM. Wiping data from these storage media is much more difficult. If the information on it is highly sensitive, then physical destruction is a must.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: Yes, the software you listed—Active@ KillDisk, Eraser, Shredit, Disk Wipe, and DBAN—are generally capable of wiping data from HDDs, USB drives, and SD cards. These tools primarily overwrite data sectors, making them effective for these traditional storage types. However, SSDs present a unique challenge due to their wear-leveling algorithms and over-provisioning. Simply overwriting data on an SSD might not erase all data blocks, as the controller can remap logical block addresses to different physical blocks. For a more secure wipe on SSDs, it's recommended to use the manufacturer's secure erase utility (often found in their SSD toolbox software) or a tool that supports the ATA Secure Erase command, as this command directly instructs the SSD controller to erase all data, including hidden and over-provisioned areas, restoring it to a factory-fresh state.

Always verify if SSDs require manufacturer-specific tools (e.g., Samsung Magician) for secure erase, as standard overwriting may not fully erase data due to wear leveling. Use caution with overwrite methods on SSDs to avoid unnecessary wear.

It's important to note that the specific capabilities and compatibility of these tools may vary, so it's always a good idea to research and test them on the specific storage devices you need to wipe before relying on them for critical data erasure tasks.

question:
How to disable the use of HTTP verb tunneling using HTTP headers or query parameters?

The attacker request uses a trusted HTTP verb such as GET or POST, but adds request headers such as X-HTTP-Method, XHTTP-Method-Override,
X-Method-Override, or a query parameter such as _method to provide a restricted verb such as PUT or DELETE. Such a request is interpreted by the target application using the verb in the request header instead of the actual
HTTP verb.
What I figured out to restrict this is to add the following entries in web.xml:
<security-constraint> 
    <web-resource-collection> 
        <web-resource-name>WhiteList_Http_Verbs</web-resource-name> 
        <url-pattern>/*</url-pattern>
        <http-method-omission>GET</http-method-omission>
        <http-method-omission>POST</http-method-omission>
    </web-resource-collection> 
    <auth-constraint/>
</security-constraint>

According to these entries my application only allows GET and POST requests for all URL patterns and it restrict all other HTTP methods.
The security constraint will apply to all methods except those that were named in the omissions, and the constraint will apply only to the resources matched by the patterns in the constraint.
My questions:

In this case, is it going to prevent method/verb HEAD through special request headers above?
Will these entries disable the use of verb tunneling using headers such as X-HTTP-Method, XHTTP-Method-Override?
Or if this is not appropriate fix, then what should we do to prevent this issue?
original_answer:
The example configuration should block the use of the HEAD verb, assuming that the server which has this configuration is receiving the verb at all (e.g. if you have a weird proxy setup which fakes HEAD requests by making a GET call, then dropping the body, it won't have any effect).
No, other headers will still be present - the server doesn't know that the other headers are treated as verbs by the application. If you want to prevent the use of other methods passed in this way, the best option is probably to remove the headers which the application can interpret as HTTP verbs. Depending on the application, this may break some functionality - I've seen applications which use this method of passing verbs for delete functions, or to allow modifications to records. 

From your description, it sounds like the problem lies not in the use of HTTP verbs, but in how the application allows the actions they relate to to be performed, possibly with inappropriate permissions. It's hard to say without further knowledge about your application, but blocking a specific method of performing an action is likely to be less effective than blocking the action itself, within the application, unless appropriate authorisation is supplied.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: Your `web.xml` configuration using `<http-method-omission>` will effectively restrict direct use of methods other than GET and POST at the servlet container level. However, this *will not* prevent HTTP verb tunneling via custom headers like `X-HTTP-Method-Override` or query parameters like `_method`. These headers/parameters are typically processed by application-level frameworks (e.g., Spring, Ruby on Rails) *after* the servlet container has already accepted the initial GET/POST request. The container sees a valid GET/POST, passes it to the application, and then the application's framework interprets the override header, potentially executing a restricted verb. To truly prevent this, you need to configure your application framework to explicitly ignore or strip these override headers/parameters, or implement a custom filter that removes them before they reach your application's routing logic.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response:
The configuration in `web.xml` you provided restricts HTTP methods at the container level (e.g., Tomcat), allowing only GET and POST for all URLs. However, this does **not** inherently block HTTP verb tunneling via headers like `X-HTTP-Method` or query parameters like `_method`. These headers/parameters are processed **before** the container enforces method restrictions, meaning the server might still interpret them as valid method overrides (e.g., treating a POST request with `X-HTTP-Method: DELETE` as a DELETE). To fully prevent this, you must:  
1. **Disable method override parsing** in your application framework (e.g., in Spring, configure `HiddenHttpMethodFilter` to reject non-POST requests or disable it entirely).  
2. **Validate and reject** any requests containing suspicious headers/parameters (e.g., via a servlet filter or middleware).  
3. **Avoid relying on client-side method overrides** and enforce strict method validation server-side.  
The `web.xml` constraint alone is insufficient, as it only blocks direct use of restricted methods (e.g., a raw DELETE request), not indirect overrides via headers/parameters.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response:
The approach you have described, which involves adding security constraints in the web.xml file to restrict the allowed HTTP methods, can effectively prevent the use of HTTP verb tunneling using request headers or query parameters.

1. Regarding your first question, the security constraint you have provided will prevent the use of the HEAD method, as it is not explicitly listed in the `<http-method-omission>` elements. The HEAD method will be restricted, along with any other HTTP methods not listed in the omissions.

2. Yes, the security constraint you have implemented will also disable the use of verb tunneling using headers such as `X-HTTP-Method`, `X-HTTP-Method-Override`, or similar. Since the application will only allow the specified HTTP methods (GET and POST), any attempt to use a different method through a header or query parameter will be blocked.

3. The approach you have described is an appropriate and effective fix to prevent HTTP verb tunneling. By explicitly defining the allowed HTTP methods in the security constraint, you are ensuring that the application only accepts the intended HTTP verbs, regardless of any attempt to override them through request headers or query parameters.

To further enhance the security, you can also consider implementing additional measures, such as:

- Validating and sanitizing all user input, including request headers and query parameters, to prevent injection attacks.
- Implementing a whitelist-based approach for allowed HTTP methods, rather than a blacklist-based approach, to ensure that any new HTTP methods introduced in the future are also properly handled.
- Logging and monitoring any attempts to use unauthorized HTTP methods, as this could be an indication of an ongoing attack.

By combining the security constraint in the web.xml file with these additional security measures, you can effectively mitigate the risks associated with HTTP verb tunneling and strengthen the overall security of your application.

question:
SEH overwrite attack details

If there are canaries on stack, why does not the exception handler check the integrity of canaries? Why does it continue executing a manipulated exception handler?
Will corrupting a canary trigger an exception? Is this a common way to trigger an exception at SEH overwrite attacks?
Why does the attack have to set the handler address to "pop-pop-ret", then jumping to the destination address, e.g., inserted shell code? I mean, why not set the handler address directly to the destination address?
I understand there are mitigation now against such attacks, I still want to know more details. Thanks. 
Ref: https://www.exploit-db.com/docs/17505.pdf
original_answer:
The exception handler does not check the integrity of the canaries since the check code is called only when the function terminates. The attacker sould make sure that before the program reaches that code, an exception should be thrown!
Without further protections, the program continues to execute a manipulated exception handler because it does not know that it is malformed.
Corrupting a canary will not trigger an exception, the program will not know that the canary has changed until the integrity check. Overflowing a buffer, on the other hand, MIGHT trigger an exception (If, for example, the attacker overruns a pointer and points it to an area that is unreadable/unwriteable and then tries to read/write with that pointer).
Assuming that the shellcode is on the stack, we do not place the address of the shellcode in the address of the exception handler because Windows has a some basic defence mechanism that prevents exceptions from jumping to addresses on the stack. SEH was abused commonly and repeatedly, and so it was created.
Therfore, we use pop, pop, ret because we can easily find this code in the text segment (an area the exception handler CAN jump to).
For more details about the mitigation techniques please read about:

SafeSEH which defines exact addresses that allow exceptions to jump to them.
SEHOP which verifies the integrity of the exception handler list.

I also asked a question on the subject, you might want to take a look at it
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: SEH overwrite attacks exploit structured exception handling to gain control of program execution. While stack canaries protect against buffer overflows by detecting stack corruption, they are typically checked *before* an exception handler is invoked. If a canary is corrupted, it will usually trigger a program termination or an exception *itself*, but this happens during function epilogue, not during the exception handling process. Therefore, the exception handler doesn't check canary integrity because its role is to handle exceptions that have *already occurred*, not to prevent them from occurring due to stack corruption. Corrupting a canary *can* trigger an exception, but it's not the primary method for initiating an SEH overwrite; rather, it's a separate vulnerability that might lead to a crash before the SEH overwrite can fully execute. The "pop-pop-ret" gadget is crucial because the exception dispatcher pushes two values onto the stack (the exception record and the context record) before calling the SEH handler. The "pop-pop-ret" sequence effectively discards these two values, aligning the stack pointer to the return address, which is then controlled by the attacker to jump to their shellcode. Directly setting the handler address to the shellcode would result in the shellcode being executed with an unaligned stack, likely causing a crash.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response: SEH (Structured Exception Handling) overwrite attacks exploit the Windows exception handling mechanism by corrupting the SEH chain to redirect execution flow. Canaries on the stack are designed to detect stack overflows, but the exception handler itself does not validate canaries during exception propagation. Instead, canary checks occur **after** the handler is invoked, typically during stack unwinding or when the system verifies the integrity of the SEH record. Attackers overwrite the SEH record before the canary is checked, allowing malicious handlers to execute unchecked. Corrupting a canary **does** trigger an exception, but this is not a common method in SEH overwrites because the attack relies on overwriting the handler first, bypassing the canary check until after execution has already been redirected. The use of a "pop-pop-ret" gadget in the handler address is necessary to **chain control flow**: when the exception is thrown, the handler address is popped into EIP, and the gadget adjusts the stack to point to the attacker's shellcode. Directly setting the handler to the shellcode address would fail because the SEH handler must conform to a specific function signature (e.g., `__except_handler`), and invalid addresses are rejected by modern mitigations like SafeSEH or ASLR. Mitigations such as SafeSEH (validating handler addresses), ASLR (randomizing memory), and stack canaries (detecting corruption) collectively reduce the feasibility of such attacks, but historical vulnerabilities exploited these timing gaps.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response:
In a SEH (Structured Exception Handling) overwrite attack, the attacker aims to overwrite the exception handler pointer on the stack with a malicious address. Even if the stack is protected by canaries, the exception handler does not typically check the integrity of the canaries. This is because the exception handler is designed to execute quickly and efficiently, and performing additional checks on the stack could introduce performance overhead. As a result, the manipulated exception handler continues to execute, allowing the attacker to gain control of the program's execution flow.

Corrupting a canary does not necessarily trigger an exception, as the canary check is typically performed before the exception handler is invoked. However, the attacker can leverage the SEH overwrite vulnerability to trigger an exception, which then allows them to execute the manipulated exception handler.

The attacker sets the handler address to "pop-pop-ret" instead of directly to the destination address (e.g., inserted shellcode) because this technique allows for more flexibility and reliability. The "pop-pop-ret" sequence effectively pops the return address off the stack, which then allows the attacker to jump to the desired destination address. This approach is more reliable than directly setting the handler address to the shellcode, as it can be more resilient to stack layout changes or other runtime conditions.

Mitigations against SEH overwrite attacks have been developed, such as Control Flow Guard (CFG) and Stack Cookies (canaries), which aim to detect and prevent these types of attacks. However, attackers continue to explore new techniques to bypass these defenses, and the field of cybersecurity remains an ongoing arms race between attackers and defenders.
meta-llama/llama-3.2-1b-instruct_respons: The canary mechanism is a common technique used in SEH overwrite attacks to detect and exploit buffer overflows. The idea is to replace the return address of the original handler with a new address that points to a malicious code segment, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary code. This is done by setting the return address to a location that is not the original return address, and then executing the original handler. This allows the attacker to overwrite the return address and execute the malicious code. The canary is then checked by the original handler, which will jump to the new return address, allowing the attacker to execute the malicious code. This technique is not foolproof, as it relies on the attacker not to overwrite the return address correctly, and it can be mitigated by using techniques such as padding the canary with zeros or using a different return address. However, it is still a common technique used in SEH overwrite attacks, and it can be used to execute arbitrary code.

question:
Are there any fast Homomorphic Obfuscuation techniques available?

Assuming that cryptography falls into one of three areas:

Awfully weak protocols and algorithms, that are trivial to break with a low amount of effort and resources
Cryptographically strong protocols and algorithms that require more time than the lifetime of the Universe to actually crack
The grey area of unproven, academically hard, or where anyone with a modest amount of resources can't break.

Question

Are there any weak, but imperfect homomorphic algorithms or approaches that are fast enough to be used in modern applications?

I'm not sure at what point weak cryptography is considered "obfuscation", but I hope that the relative investment in cryptography has a corresponding improvement in security of my plain text data.
original_answer:
There have been a few implementations of fully homomorphic encryption (2010).  Fully homomorphic means it can perform addition AND multiplication (as opposed to partially homomorphic).
The answer to your question though relies on what you consider "weak" and sufficient for security.  Does the encryption have to stand up for 100 years, or is it OK if it's cracked a week from now (short term secrets such as military troop movements)?
Anyway, homomorphic encryption really isn't ready for prime time.  It's mostly a research area at the moment.  The current "implementations" are very academic with little documentation and haven't been vetted (using encryption which hasn't been tested extensively is a fools errand).
Here's a link anyway if you want see what the current state of the art implementation looks like http://github.com/shaih/HElib/

question:
Is a responsible disclosure for hardware-based vulnerabilities even possible?

In the last decade side-channel attacks like fault injection attacks (e.g., voltage glitching attacks) have been used to bypass JTAG locks or read-out memory protections. Such vulnerabilities might not be easy to prevent. They can be caused by the hardware design or by the firmware in use. Therefore, in order to fix such vulnerabilities, vendors might need to change hardware design of their products so that the vulnerability cannot be easily fixed in customer products without replacing the component.
Is such a thing as "responsible disclosure" even possible, given that fixing such a vulnerability might need a hardware redesign and a replacement of components?
Would it therefore not be unethical to release any public information after 90 days?
original_answer:
The idea of a responsible disclosure is to protect the customers both in the short term and the long term. What this exactly means in terms of disclosure timelines and details depends on how severe the vulnerability is, how fast it can be fixed and how fast the fixes can be rolled out by the customers. There is no single approach to responsible disclosure which works for all cases.
In the long term it is good if the affected products get fixed or at least newer products don't have the same vulnerability. This might take longer with hardware based vulnerabilities than with purely software. For fixing the problem the manufacturer needs to know the problem, so disclosing it to the manufacturer is a good thing. If it is a more generic vulnerability affecting multiple manufacturers or even standards, then it needs an even wider disclosure.
But customers need to protect themselves in the short term already. For this they might not need all the details about the vulnerability or even working exploits, but customers need enough details to determine their risks and mitigate them. How this mitigation will be done with vulnerabilities in the hardware depends on how the product is used by the customer, i.e. it might mean to add better physical protection around the product or in the worst case stop using the product completely.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: Responsible disclosure for hardware-based vulnerabilities, especially those requiring hardware redesign, presents unique challenges but remains ethically crucial. While a 90-day disclosure timeline might be impractical for a full hardware fix and replacement, the core principle of responsible disclosure—notifying the vendor privately to allow for mitigation efforts before public release—is still applicable. Vendors can implement interim software patches, firmware updates, or provide guidance to customers on mitigating risks, even if a complete hardware solution is years away. Public disclosure, even after an extended period, serves to inform users of potential risks, allowing them to make informed decisions about product usage and security. The ethical dilemma lies in balancing the need for public awareness with the potential for exploitation, which often necessitates a flexible and collaborative approach between researchers and vendors, potentially extending disclosure timelines beyond the typical 90 days with mutual agreement.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response: Responsible disclosure for hardware-based vulnerabilities is possible but complex, as fixing such issues often requires hardware redesigns or component replacements, which are resource-intensive and time-consuming. Unlike software patches, hardware fixes may not be feasible for existing products, leaving vendors with limited options. However, responsible disclosure still applies by allowing vendors time to assess risks, develop mitigations (e.g., firmware updates, design changes in future models), or communicate with customers about workarounds. A rigid 90-day timeline may be impractical here, as vendors might need extended periods to address hardware flaws. Ethical disclosure balances transparency with practicality: researchers should collaborate with vendors to determine feasible timelines, prioritize user safety, and avoid premature public exposure that could enable exploitation before mitigations are available. While full resolution may not always be possible, disclosure remains ethical if it promotes accountability and spurs long-term design improvements, even if immediate fixes are unattainable.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response: Responsible disclosure for hardware-based vulnerabilities can be challenging, as the nature of these vulnerabilities often requires hardware-level changes to address them effectively. Side-channel attacks, such as fault injection attacks, can exploit vulnerabilities in the hardware design or firmware, making it difficult to provide a quick fix without a hardware redesign. In such cases, the vendor may need to replace the affected components, which can be a complex and costly process for both the vendor and the customers. Releasing public information about these vulnerabilities after a 90-day disclosure period may not be considered ethical, as it could potentially expose users to risks without a viable solution being readily available. The responsible approach would be for the vendor to work closely with security researchers to understand the vulnerability, assess the impact, and develop a long-term solution that involves a hardware redesign or a replacement of the affected components. This process may take longer than the typical 90-day disclosure period, but it ensures that the vulnerability is addressed in a way that protects the end-users without unnecessarily exposing them to potential exploitation.
meta-llama/llama-3.2-1b-instruct_respons: A responsible disclosure of hardware-based vulnerabilities, such as those caused by side-channel attacks, is indeed possible, but it's a complex and challenging process. The fact that the vulnerability can be exploited by attackers through hardware means makes it difficult to prevent the vulnerability without redesigning the hardware. However, responsible disclosure can still occur. In this case, the disclosure process typically involves a collaborative effort between the vendor and the security community, where the vendor provides a fixed patch or a workaround to the vulnerability, and the community reviews and tests it to ensure its safety and effectiveness. This approach allows the vendor to minimize the impact of the vulnerability on their customers while still providing a solution. As for the 90-day window, it's not necessarily unethical to release public information about a vulnerability after 90 days, as it can help prevent further exploitation and allow the vendor to fix the vulnerability before it can be exploited by malicious actors. However, it's essential to ensure that the disclosure process is transparent, and the vendor provides a clear timeline for patching the vulnerability and addressing any concerns.

question:
Is it harmful to allow http requests with very long querystrings in IIS?

I am developing a website with a search feature that could generate HTTP requests that are very long (up to 15,000) characters. However, IIS has a default limit of 2048 characters for querystrings. This can be easily changed in a web.config file using the settings listed below.
system.web > httpRuntime > maxRequestLength
system.web > httpRuntime > maxUrlLength
system.webServer > security > requestFiltering > maxQueryString
system.webServer > security > requestFiltering > maxUrl
My question is whether there are any security concerns that I should be aware of if I allow very long querystrings in HTTP requests?
Update: I should add a few bits of info.

The website will only be available to paid users who have signed into the website. 
The likelihood that anyone would create a search with more than 2048 characters in the url is VERY slim, but theoretically possible.
original_answer:
15,000 character URLs isn't going to work very well cross-browser: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/417142/what-is-the-maximum-length-of-a-url
Assuming you already know that, in an ASP.NET application, a 16,000 character limit will increase your vulnerability to request-queue-buffer exhaustion attacks 8x. Now, 8x is not that significant compared to better DoS attacks generally open on an ASP.NET website.
Regular expression matching is not probably a big issue. Most regexp attacks work equally well with 64-character and 64,000 character strings - they exploit an exponential backtrack pattern, and your request timeout makes the URL length quite unimportant. On the upside, most of these kinds of issues will show themselves quickly in performance issues. Also, regexes generally are only applied to the path, not the querystring.
Path regexes are generally quite fast and usually exit the match at the first integer comparison, although there are exceptions.
If you raise the limit, just ensure all URL rewriting, routes, path matching, and pre-HandleRequest events are efficient, have fast exit paths, and don't do any high-latency work like disk or db access.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: While increasing IIS query string limits might seem convenient for your search feature, it introduces several security and performance concerns. Extremely long query strings can be exploited in Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks, where an attacker sends numerous large requests to exhaust server resources, leading to service unavailability. They can also increase the attack surface for SQL injection or cross-site scripting (XSS) if input validation isn't robustly implemented across all parameters. Furthermore, logging and monitoring become more challenging with excessively long URLs, potentially obscuring malicious activity. Even with authenticated users, a compromised account could be used to launch such attacks. Consider alternative approaches like using POST requests for large search queries, implementing server-side session storage for complex search parameters, or breaking down very complex searches into multiple, smaller requests to mitigate these risks.

While increasing limits via `maxQueryString` and `maxUrl` settings accommodates your search feature, excessively long URLs may expose vulnerabilities such as buffer overflow attacks, which could crash the server or allow code execution if IIS or dependent components mishandle the input. Additionally, long query strings might be exploited for URL injection, encoding abuse (e.g., nested encodings), or excessive resource consumption, potentially leading to denial-of-service (DoS) scenarios. Since the site is for paid users, the risk of malicious actors is lower, but input validation and sanitization remain critical to prevent injection attacks (e.g., SQLi, XSS) or data leakage through logs/cache. Consider using POST requests for large payloads instead of GET, as query strings are inherently less secure for sensitive or voluminous data. If you must use GET, ensure strict validation, limit query string length to a reasonable threshold (e.g., 16,384 characters), and monitor for anomalies to mitigate risks.

This can be especially problematic if the website is under heavy load or if the attacker is targeting the server with a large number of these requests.

Additionally, long query strings can also increase the risk of URL-based attacks, such as SQL injection or cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities. Attackers may attempt to exploit the increased length of the query string to inject malicious code or bypass security measures.

To mitigate these risks, it's recommended to implement additional security measures, such as input validation and sanitization, to ensure that the user-provided search terms are properly validated and do not contain any malicious content. Additionally, you should monitor the website's performance and logs for any suspicious activity or unusual traffic patterns that may indicate an attempted DoS attack or other security breaches.

Overall, while increasing the default limits in the web.config file can accommodate your search feature, it's crucial to carefully consider the potential security implications and implement appropriate safeguards to protect your website and its users.

question:
Unpredictability vs Predictability

Unpredictability and Predictability in security.
I am tasked to come up on which is more important in terms of security, if possible, on cyber security.
After reading through several articles on cyber security, i noticed many of the examples used falls under predictability, as most security engineers are dealing with known threats throughout the cyber world, however, is it possible to classify them as part of unpredictability as well. Since it is impossible to know when an attacker will attack, how he/she will attack, how much resources he/she will use.
Like a vaccine, you would not know when you will get sick, therefore, having regular vaccine shots will keep you safer, in a sense. 
However, similar to cyber security, if we are to discuss about unpredictability, we will not know what new kinds of attacks are developed and can our current defending system defend against such new attacks.
In my opinion, unpredictability is without a doubt the more important factor compared to predictability.  How am i to go about debating and explaining about this? Are there any references that I can look into to understand more about this factor?
P.S
Such a good question yet it is closed
original_answer:
What might help you is to look through InfoSec Risk Frameworks (NIST 800-39, 800-30 r1, ISO 31000, etc.) and see how they deal with the concept of "Likelihood" in terms of assessing risk. 
You might also gain some insight by looking at Bayes Theorem. It's a massive topic, but it is a great way to gain a foothold in understanding how the unpredictable affects the predictable.
You also have to define what you want to do with the unpredictable/predictable. Why make the distinction? How does it help an InfoSec professional? If you can't predict something, how do you suppose to account for it.

While many security measures address predictable, known threats (like patching vulnerabilities or using signature-based antivirus), the true challenge lies in the inherent unpredictability of attacker behavior and novel attack vectors. You're right that it's impossible to know precisely *when* or *how* an attacker will strike, or what zero-day exploits they might leverage. This unpredictability necessitates a proactive, adaptive security posture. Focusing solely on predictability leaves organizations vulnerable to unknown threats. Therefore, emphasizing unpredictability in your debate is key. You can argue that while predictable defenses are foundational, true resilience comes from anticipating the unpredictable through threat intelligence, behavioral analytics, anomaly detection, and robust incident response plans. References on "adaptive security architecture," "zero-trust models," and "threat hunting" will provide excellent support for your argument, as these concepts are designed to address the unpredictable nature of modern cyber threats.
qwen/qwen3-32b_response: In cybersecurity, both unpredictability and predictability play critical roles, but their importance depends on the context. Predictability is essential for defending against known threats (e.g., malware signatures, common attack vectors) through proactive measures like patching, firewalls, and threat intelligence. However, unpredictability becomes paramount when addressing novel or evolving threats (e.g., zero-day exploits, AI-driven attacks) that bypass traditional defenses. While predictability allows for structured defense strategies, unpredictability demands resilience through adaptive systems, behavioral analysis, and AI-driven anomaly detection. Your analogy to vaccines is apt: known threats can be mitigated with preparedness, but unknown threats require robust, flexible defenses. To debate this, emphasize that while predictability provides a foundation, unpredictability drives innovation in security (e.g., zero-trust architecture, deception technologies). References like NIST’s Cybersecurity Framework, the MITRE ATT&CK matrix, and research on "unknown unknowns" in threat modeling can strengthen your argument. Ultimately, a balance is ideal, but unpredictability often dictates long-term security resilience.
anthropic/claude-3-haiku_response:

On one hand, security professionals rely heavily on predictability to defend against known threats and vulnerabilities. By anticipating and preparing for common attack vectors, they can implement robust security measures and protocols to mitigate risks. This predictability-based approach allows for the development of effective security solutions, such as firewalls, antivirus software, and intrusion detection systems, which can reliably protect against well-understood threats.

However, the cybersecurity landscape is constantly evolving, with new and increasingly sophisticated attacks emerging constantly. This unpredictability is a significant challenge, as security teams must be agile and adaptable to respond to novel threats. Attackers often exploit unknown vulnerabilities or employ innovative techniques that bypass traditional security measures. In this context, unpredictability becomes a critical factor, as security professionals must anticipate and prepare for the unexpected, investing in proactive threat intelligence, incident response planning, and continuous security monitoring.

Ultimately, a successful cybersecurity strategy requires a balance between predictability and unpredictability. While predictability-based defenses are essential for addressing known threats, the ability to adapt to and mitigate unpredictable attacks is equally crucial. Security teams must combine their understanding of past and present threats with the agility to respond to emerging challenges, leveraging a range of tools, techniques, and best practices to maintain a robust and resilient security posture.
meta-llama/llama-3.2-1b-instruct_respons: The age-old debate between unpredictability and predictability in cybersecurity. While predictability is crucial in terms of identifying and mitigating known threats, unpredictability is essential in anticipating and responding to emerging threats. In other words, predictability is about knowing what to expect, while unpredictability is about knowing what's coming. Cybersecurity engineers and researchers often refer to this as the "unknown unknowns" problem, where the unknown is the unknown unknown. To debate this, one can cite examples such as the rise of advanced persistent threats (APTs), which are highly sophisticated and difficult to detect, or the increasing use of AI-powered attacks that can adapt and evolve rapidly. References to this can be found in books like "The Zero Trust Era" by Peter Moynihan and "The Hacker's Handbook" by Jon Erickson. Additionally, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) defines predictability as "the ability to anticipate and respond to a wide range of potential threats" and unpredictability as "the ability to anticipate and respond to unexpected threats".

"Implementing and using suitable authentication techniques, not disclosing sensitive information at log-on time, data entry validation, protection against brute-force attacks, logging, not transmitting passwords in clear over the network, session inactivity timeouts, and access time restrictions."

question:
What is WebRTC and how can I protect myself against leaking information that doesn't need to be shared?

What is WebRTC and what is it intended to be used for?
According to this Browser Leaks Test for WebRTC and IP Leaks, I am currently leaking IP information for IP addresses that I do not believe anybody on the internet needs to know, ever. 
I installed a plugin for Google Chrome (WebRTC Block) which apparently disables WebRTC - the Browser Leaks page reports that WebRTC is disabled but the IP Leaks link still lists IPv4 IP addresses in the 192.168.x range.
How can I protect myself against WebRTC leaking information that doesn't need to be shared? Is it possible to keep WebRTC enabled for the purposes it was designed for without revealing IP information?
original_answer:
WebRTC stands for Web Real Time Communication.  It's used for most web related voice, video, or file sharing capabilities.  Specifically instances of communication where you are directly connecting to your peer through your browser.  Some examples could be web-games that let you play tic-tac-toe with a friend.  Sharing files directly through a browser to another peer.  Most browsers have native support for WebRTC except for Internet Explorer and Safari.  It uses encryption for all its communications, but as you've found there's a huge vulnerability.
It seems that Windows and FreeBSD are the vulnerable operating systems.  I'm currently running a Debian based distro, and it does not seem to be vulnerable based on the two links you've provided.
It does not appear that you can only block the leaked IP vulnerability and use WebRTC for its intended use.  

Mitigations
I'm not sure which plugin you've installed to disable WebRTC.  This article states that both WebRTC Block and ScriptSafe extensions for Chrome will stop the vulnerability.  For Chrome make sure that all Chrome processes have been closed after you've installed the extension.  Sometimes the Chrome sandboxes aren't properly closed when the browser window is.  This will ensure the new extension has taken effect properly.
For FireFox the NoScript add-on should block the request.  You can also disable some settings with the following steps:

Type about:config in the address bar
Find the setting media.peerconnection.enabled and set it to false
Find the setting media.navigator.enabled and set it to false

I would not suggest using Internet Explorer as a mitigation... a leaked IP would probably be the least of your worries.  Switching OSs is an option, but probably a tedious one.
google/gemini-2.5-flash_response: WebRTC (Web Real-Time Communication) is an open-source project enabling real-time communication capabilities like video, audio, and data sharing directly within web browsers without requiring external plugins. It's primarily intended for applications like video conferencing, online gaming, and live streaming. The IP addresses you're seeing (192.168.x) are likely your local network IPs, which are not directly routable on the public internet and are less of a privacy concern than public IPs. To protect against WebRTC leaks of public IP information while potentially keeping WebRTC enabled for its intended uses, you can employ a VPN that specifically routes WebRTC traffic through its secure tunnel. Some VPNs offer WebRTC leak protection features. Alternatively, browser extensions like "WebRTC Network Limiter" can restrict WebRTC to only use your public IP address, preventing local IP disclosure, or you can configure your browser's settings (e.g., in Firefox, `media.peerconnection.ice.obfuscate_host_addresses` to `true`) to mask local IPs. However, completely disabling WebRTC via extensions like "WebRTC Block" is the most straightforward way to prevent all WebRTC-related IP leaks if you don't require its functionality.

To mitigate leaks, disable WebRTC via browser extensions (e.g., WebRTC Block) or built-in settings (e.g., Chrome’s `chrome://flags/#enable-webrtc`). If using a VPN, ensure it routes WebRTC traffic through the tunnel by configuring split-tunneling or using a browser that enforces strict IP masking (e.g., Firefox with `media.peerconnection.enabled` set to `false`). The 192.168.x IPs you see are private (internal) addresses, not a concern, but public IP leaks require blocking WebRTC entirely or using a browser that isolates WebRTC traffic. While WebRTC can be used securely with proper configuration, full protection often necessitates disabling it unless critical for specific applications.

It was designed to facilitate features like video conferencing, voice calling, and file sharing directly between users' devices without the need for an intermediary server. While WebRTC is a powerful technology, it can potentially leak sensitive information, such as your local IP address, which can be used to identify your location or network. It's primarily used for secure, in-band communication between browsers, but it can also be used for out-of-band communication, such as for data exchange between browsers. To protect yourself against WebRTC leaking information that doesn't need to be shared, you can use WebRTC's built-in features like WebRTC's "Secure Real-Time Communication" mode, which encrypts all WebRTC traffic.
